case.input: help doctors make better decisions at hospitals

complianceFound: FDA SaMD Class II (Software as a Medical Device)
notes: vague input but no gaps identified

raw.use_case_type: AI-assisted clinical decision support system (CDSS) for diagnosis and treatment recommendations

performance_goals.metric: Model AUC-ROC on held-out validation set
performance_goals.rationale: Ensures the AI provides discriminative power comparable to expert clinicians for disease detection.
Low latency is critical for real-time use at the point of care without disrupting workflow.
High satisfaction correlates with adoption and sustained use of the CDSS in clinical practice.

compliance: FDA SaMD Class II (Software as a Medical Device)
constraints.hardware: Hospital‑grade GPU servers (NVIDIA A100 or newer) with ≥32 GB GPU memory; optional edge inference via NVIDIA Jetson AGX for low‑latency sites
constraints.scale: Support up to 500 concurrent clinicians, processing ~10 000 patient records per day across multiple departments
other: Provide model explainability (e.g., SHAP values) for each recommendation
Maintain immutable audit logs of all AI‑generated suggestions and clinician actions

inferred_requirements.requirement: Real‑time integration with existing EHR systems via HL7/FHIR standards to pull patient vitals, labs, and medication data
inferred_requirements.reason: Without seamless data flow, the CDSS cannot generate timely, context‑aware recommendations, limiting its utility.
Clinicians need to understand the rationale behind AI suggestions to trust and act on them; regulators also require transparency for SaMD approval.
inferred_requirements.requirement: Model explainability and uncertainty quantification (e.g., feature importance, confidence intervals) built into the user interface
Continuous monitoring and retraining pipeline to detect concept drift and update model performance
inferred_requirements.reason: Clinical data distributions shift over time (new protocols, seasonal outbreaks); without monitoring, model accuracy can degrade silently.

conflicts.conflict: Low latency target (≤200 ms) vs. high model complexity needed to achieve AUC ≥0.88
conflicts.suggestion: Apply model distillation, quantization, or use TensorRT‑optimized inference to retain accuracy while meeting latency goals.

conflicts.conflict: Data privacy regulations (HIPAA/GDPR) vs. need for large, diverse training datasets across institutions
conflicts.suggestion: Adopt federated learning or secure multi‑party computation approaches, combined with rigorous de‑identification, to train on multi‑site data without exposing PHI.

raw.summary: The goal is to deploy a real‑time, explainable AI clinical decision support system that integrates with hospital EHRs, delivers diagnostic recommendations with AUC ≥0.88 and sub‑200 ms latency, and achieves clinician satisfaction scores above 4.2/5, all while meeting HIPAA, GDPR, and FDA SaMD requirements.

case.input: Build a HIPAA-compliant clinical decision support system for a 500-bed hospital. Process EHR data, integrate with Epic via HL7 FHIR, provide triage recommendations with <500ms p95 latency, support 10K queries/day, include audit logs, de-identify all training data.

performance_goals.rationale: Ensures timely triage recommendations for clinicians at point of care.
Critical for continuous clinical operations and patient safety.
Guarantees HIPAA compliance by removing protected health information from all training data.
Required for HIPAA audit trails, accountability, and forensic analysis.

constraints.hardware: On-premise private cloud or HIPAA-eligible public cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP) with GPU inference nodes (e.g., NVIDIA A100 or T4)
constraints.scale: Support 500-bed hospital, average 10K queries/day (~0.12 QPS), peak 2x average (~0.24 QPS) while maintaining latency SLA
other: HL7 FHIR R4 integration with Epic
Role-based access control (RBAC)
Encryption at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+)

inferred_requirements.requirement: Continuous model performance monitoring and drift detection
inferred_requirements.reason: Ensures triage recommendations remain accurate as patient populations, disease prevalence, and clinical guidelines evolve over time.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Explainability interface for clinicians (e.g., SHAP values, attention highlights)
inferred_requirements.reason: Builds trust, supports clinical validation, and meets regulatory expectations for transparent decision support.

gaps.gap: Target clinical accuracy or performance metric for triage recommendations (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, AUC)
gaps.suggestion: Define acceptable accuracy thresholds based on clinical risk (e.g., >=85% sensitivity for high-acuity cases).

gaps.gap: Audit log retention period and storage requirements
gaps.suggestion: Specify retention duration (e.g., 6 years per HIPAA) and secure, tamper-evident storage solution.

gaps.gap: Model retraining and versioning schedule
gaps.suggestion: Establish frequency (e.g., quarterly) and process for validating new model versions before deployment.

conflicts.conflict: Ultra-low latency (<500ms p95) vs. using complex deep learning models that may increase inference time
conflicts.suggestion: Consider model optimization techniques (quantization, pruning, distillation) or hardware acceleration to meet latency without sacrificing accuracy.

conflicts.conflict: Complete de-identification of all training data vs. potential loss of clinically relevant features that could reduce model accuracy
conflicts.suggestion: Apply advanced de-identification methods (e.g., synthetic data generation, differential privacy) that preserve statistical utility while removing PHI.

raw.summary: A HIPAA-compliant, real-time clinical decision support system for a 500-bed hospital that ingests EHR data via Epic HL7 FHIR, delivers triage recommendations under 500ms latency at 95th percentile, handles 10K daily queries, maintains comprehensive audit logs, and ensures all training data is fully de-identified, while incorporating model monitoring, explainability, and robust security controls.

raw.use_case_type: Real-time transaction monitoring and anomaly detection

performance_goals.rationale: Ensures most fraudulent transactions are caught to minimize financial loss.
Kills unnecessary customer friction and operational overhead from false alerts.
Allows real‑time blocking or challenge before settlement, preserving user experience.
Supports high‑volume retail banking traffic during peak periods (e.g., paydays, holidays).

performance_goals.target: Full refresh ≤ 24 h, incremental updates ≤ 1 h
performance_goals.rationale: Keeps the model current with evolving fraud patterns and new threat vectors.

constraints.hardware: NVIDIA GPU-accelerated servers (e.g., A100 or T4) for model inference; CPU‑only fallback for preprocessing; optional Triton Inference Server.
constraints.scale: Daily transaction volume: 100M–1B+; peak concurrent users: up to 500k; geographic distribution: multi‑region.
other: Model explainability for audit trails (e.g., SHAP values per alert)
End‑to‑end encryption of transaction data in transit and at rest
Role‑based access control (RBAC) and audit logging for all model/pipeline changes

inferred_requirements.requirement: Real-time streaming ingest (e.g., Apache Kafka or Pulsar) with exactly‑once semantics
inferred_requirements.reason: Fraud detection must operate on the live transaction stream; any loss or duplication could miss fraud or cause double‑counting.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Feature store with low-latency retrieval (e.g., Feast or custom Redis-backed store)
inferred_requirements.reason: Consistent, up-to-date customer and transaction features (velocity, geo, device) are critical for accurate model scoring.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Feedback loop with automated labeling from fraud investigations and chargeback data
inferred_requirements.reason: Supervised models decay without fresh labeled examples; a closed-loop ensures continuous improvement.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Model governance framework (versioning, A/B testing, canary rollout)
inferred_requirements.reason: Regulatory compliance and risk management require traceable model changes and performance validation before full deployment.

gaps.gap: Transaction volume and peak TPS expectations not specified
gaps.suggestion: Clarify expected daily transaction count and peak load to size hardware and streaming infrastructure.

gaps.gap: Acceptable cost per false positive (e.g., operational review cost) not defined
gaps.suggestion: Define a cost‑benefit target to balance recall vs. precision based on business impact.

gaps.gap: Geographic/jurisdictional scope for data residency and regulations not given
gaps.suggestion: Identify primary markets (e.g., EU, US, APAC) to tailor compliance controls and data localization.

gaps.gap: Latency tolerance for downstream actions (e.g., block, challenge, or allow) not detailed
gaps.suggestion: Specify whether a hard decline must happen within 200 ms or if a slightly higher latency is acceptable for step‑up authentication.

conflicts.metric: High Recall vs. Low False Positive Rate
conflicts.conflict: Increasing recall often raises false positives, potentially violating the ≤0.5% FPR target.
conflicts.suggestion: Employ cost‑sensitive learning or threshold tuning; consider a two‑stage model (high‑recall screening + precision‑focused second stage).

conflicts.conflict: More complex models (e.g., deep ensembles) improve detection but may exceed the 200 ms latency budget.
conflicts.suggestion: Use model distillation or GPU‑optimized inference (TensorRT) to meet latency while preserving accuracy.

raw.summary: The goal is to build a real‑time, GPU‑accelerated fraud detection system for bank transactions that catches at least 95 % of fraud while keeping false alerts below 0.5 %, processing each transaction in under 200 ms and sustaining peak throughput of 15k TPS. The solution must satisfy PCI DSS, GDPR, AML and other regulatory constraints, incorporate a streaming feature store, feedback‑loop labeling, explainability, and robust model governance, and be scalable to hundreds of millions of daily transactions across multiple regions.

case.input: Real-time credit card fraud detection at 50K TPS, <20ms p99 decision latency, integrate with Kafka event stream, SOC2 compliance, use graph features, deploy on A100 GPUs, explainability for declined transactions, weekly retraining on last 90 days of data.

raw.use_case_type: Online ML inference with streaming feature engineering and explainability

performance_goals.rationale: Matches the required peak transaction volume for the credit card processing pipeline.

performance_goals.metric: Decision latency (p99 end-to-end from Kafka consume to fraud decision output)
performance_goals.rationale: Ensures real-time blocking decisions do not degrade user experience or transaction flow.
High discriminative power is needed to catch fraud while keeping false alarms low.

performance_goals.metric: Explainability latency (p99 additional time for SHAP values on declined transactions)
performance_goals.rationale: Provides timely explanations for compliance and customer service without breaking the overall latency budget.

performance_goals.metric: Weekly retraining job completion time on A100 cluster
performance_goals.rationale: Allows timely model updates with the latest 90‑day data while keeping training windows short enough to avoid interference with inference.

constraints.hardware: NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs (minimum 8 GPUs for inference scaling; additional GPUs for training)
constraints.scale: Peak load 50K TPS, burstable to 75K TPS; horizontal scaling via Kubernetes pods
other: Kafka 2.+ event stream integration (exactly-once semantics)
Feature store for real-time graph features
Data retention: 90 days of raw transactions for training
Weekly retraining cadence

inferred_requirements.requirement: Real-time graph feature computation service (e.g., GPU‑accelerated Neo4j/TigerGraph or pre‑computed node embeddings with ANN lookup)
inferred_requirements.reason: Graph features are central to the model but must be fetched within the latency budget; a dedicated low‑latency feature service prevents CPU‑GPU bottlenecks.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Model serving platform with dynamic batching and GPU utilization (NVIDIA Triton Inference Server)
inferred_requirements.reason: To achieve 50K TPS with <20ms p99 latency, the serving system must efficiently batch requests and keep GPUs saturated.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Monitoring, alerting, and observability stack (Prometheus/Grafana + Loki) for data drift, model performance, and system health
inferred_requirements.reason: SOC2 compliance and production reliability require continuous visibility into latency, error rates, and feature/distribution shifts.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Encryption at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+) plus role‑based access control for Kafka, feature store, and model artifacts
inferred_requirements.reason: Financial data and model assets must be protected to meet SOC2 security controls and regulatory expectations.

gaps.gap: Target fraud prevalence or class imbalance ratio (e.g., expected fraud rate per 10K transactions)
gaps.suggestion: Specify the expected positive rate to calibrate class‑weighting, evaluation metrics, and alert thresholds.

gaps.gap: Chosen explainability method (SHAP, LIME, counterfactual, or surrogate) and whether it is computed synchronously or asynchronously
gaps.suggestion: Define the technique and latency budget allocation to ensure the <20ms p99 goal remains achievable.

gaps.gap: Kafka topic partitioning scheme and consumer group sizing needed to sustain 50K TPS
gaps.suggestion: Calculate required partitions (e.g., 100 partitions @ 500 msgs/sec each) and consumer instances to avoid back‑pressure.

gaps.gap: Storage backend for feature vectors and model checkpoints (e.g., S3, EFS, or NVMe over fabric)
gaps.suggestion: Select a low‑latency, highly available store that can serve feature lookups and support rapid model rollout.

gaps.gap: Fault‑tolerance and autoscaling policy (e.g., Kubernetes HPA thresholds, pod disruption budgets)
gaps.suggestion: Detail scaling triggers based on CPU/GPU utilization and lag metrics to guarantee availability under load spikes.

conflicts.conflict: Graph feature computation may increase per‑transaction latency beyond the 20 ms p99 target if done naively on CPU
conflicts.suggestion: Pre‑compute static graph embeddings and use GPU‑accelerated approximate nearest‑neighbor lookup, or push graph ops to the GPU via libraries like cuGraph.

conflicts.conflict: Weekly retraining on 90 days of data could compete for the same A100 GPUs used for inference, risking latency spikes
conflicts.suggestion: Separate training workload onto a dedicated GPU node pool or use spot/preemptible instances, and schedule retraining during low‑traffic windows (e.g., early morning UTC).

conflicts.conflict: Providing explainability (SHAP) for every declined transaction adds extra compute that may jeopardize the latency budget
conflicts.suggestion: Compute explainability asynchronously for a sampled subset of declines, or use a lightweight surrogate model (e.g., linear approximation) that adds <1 ms overhead.

raw.summary: The project aims to build a SOC2‑compliant, real‑time credit‑card fraud detection service that ingests Kafka event streams at 50K TPS, makes sub‑20 ms p99 decisions using GPU‑accelerated graph‑enhanced models on A100 hardware, provides rapid explainability for blocked transactions, and retrains weekly on the most recent 90 days of data. Achieving this requires low‑latency feature serving, efficient Triton‑based inference, robust monitoring, and careful separation of training and inference workloads to meet performance, compliance, and operational goals.

case.input: E-commerce recommendation engine for 5M-SKU catalog, 20M monthly users, support cold-start for new items, <100ms serving latency, A/B test framework, GDPR consent handling, optimize for CTR and margin.

notes: http 500: {"error":"Goal analysis failed — check NVIDIA_API_KEY","detail":"GoalSpec failed schema validation: conflicts.1.conflict: Too small: expected string to have >=1 characters; conflicts.1.severity: Inval

case.input: make a chatbot that answers from our docs

grades.domainLabel: Enterprise Knowledge Management / Internal Documentation Q&A

raw.domain: Enterprise Knowledge Management / Internal Documentation Q&A
raw.use_case_type: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) chatbot for document-based question answering

performance_goals.metric: Answer Accuracy (percentage of correct answers against a held‑out test set)
performance_goals.rationale: Ensures the chatbot reliably extracts and synthesizes information from the source docs, meeting user trust thresholds.

performance_goals.metric: End‑to‑End Latency (time from user query to final answer)
performance_goals.rationale: Provides a responsive conversational experience; higher latency degrades usability especially in real‑time support scenarios.
Supports department‑scale adoption without degrading latency or accuracy.

performance_goals.metric: Hallucination Rate (percentage of answers containing unsupported facts)
performance_goals.rationale: Limits misinformation, critical for internal compliance and decision‑making.

constraints.hardware: On‑prem GPU servers (e.g., NVIDIA A100) or equivalent cloud GPU instances; CPU fallback for embedding preprocessing
constraints.scale: Document corpus size: up to 100 k files (~10 GB raw text); incremental updates daily
other: Must support role‑based access control (RBAC) to restrict doc visibility
Multi‑language support (English primary, optional Spanish/French)

inferred_requirements.requirement: Embedding model and vector store for semantic retrieval (e.g., Sentence‑Transformer + FAISS or Milvus)
inferred_requirements.reason: Enables fast, relevant snippet selection from large doc sets, which is foundational to RAG accuracy.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Prompt engineering pipeline with safety filters and fallback to human escalation
inferred_requirements.reason: Reduces hallucinations and ensures compliance when the model is uncertain.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Continuous ingestion pipeline (ETL) to re‑index docs on change with versioning
inferred_requirements.reason: Keeps the chatbot up‑to‑date without manual re‑training, meeting freshness expectations.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Observability stack (logging, metrics, tracing, A/B testing framework)
inferred_requirements.reason: Allows monitoring of latency, accuracy drift, and usage patterns for SLA compliance and iterative improvement.

gaps.gap: Exact document formats (PDF, HTML, Markdown, etc.) and average size
gaps.suggestion: Conduct an inventory of source docs to size the embedding and storage requirements.

gaps.gap: Update frequency and change‑rate of the documentation
gaps.suggestion: Define whether updates are batch‑daily, real‑time, or event‑driven to choose streaming vs batch indexing.

gaps.gap: User authentication method (SSO, LDAP, etc.)
gaps.suggestion: Specify identity provider to integrate RBAC and audit logging.

gaps.gap: Target deployment environment (air‑gapped, VPC, public cloud)
gaps.suggestion: Clarify infrastructure constraints to select appropriate hardware and data‑transfer mechanisms.

gaps.gap: Budget or cost ceiling for GPU usage and licensing
gaps.suggestion: Estimate expected QPS to size instances and control OPEX.

conflicts.conflict: Using larger, more accurate LLMs increases latency; stricter latency targets may force smaller models that reduce answer quality.
conflicts.suggestion: Adopt a hybrid approach: retrieve top‑k passages with a lightweight encoder, then rerank with a compact LLM; optionally cache frequent queries.

conflicts.metric: Data Privacy vs. External API Use
conflicts.conflict: If using a third‑party LLM API (e.g., GPT‑4) to improve accuracy, internal docs may leave the trust boundary, violating GDPR/ISO‑27001.
conflicts.suggestion: Host the LLM internally (e.g., NVIDIA Triton with Llama‑2/ChatGLM) or enforce strict data‑processing agreements and anonymization before API calls.

conflicts.conflict: Continuous re‑indexing can cause temporary retrieval inconsistencies, affecting accuracy; batch indexing improves stability but increases staleness.
conflicts.suggestion: Implement a dual‑index strategy: a stable baseline index refreshed nightly, plus a delta index for recent changes merged query‑time.

raw.summary: The goal is to build an enterprise‑grade RAG chatbot that lets users ask natural‑language questions and receive accurate, source‑grounded answers from the internal documentation corpus. It must meet strict accuracy (>90%) and latency (<2 s) targets while supporting at least 100 concurrent users, ensuring data privacy, and providing continuous updates with observability and access controls.

case.input: Enterprise RAG chatbot over 50GB of PDF technical manuals, citations with page numbers, respond in <3s, 500 concurrent users, SSO and RBAC for sensitive docs, hallucination rate <2% on eval set, on-premise deployment (no external API calls).

raw.use_case_type: Document-grounded conversational assistant for technical support

performance_goals.rationale: Meets user experience requirement for interactive chatbot while maintaining throughput.
Ensures the system can handle peak enterprise load without degradation.
Guarantees high trustworthiness of answers, critical for technical manuals.
Ensures the relevant manual section is fetched before generation, reducing hallucinations.
Provides verifiable traceability to source documents for audit and compliance.

constraints.hardware: On-premise server with >= 2x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs, >= 256GB RAM, NVMe SSD storage for vector index
constraints.scale: Corpus size ~50GB PDF (~5M tokens), peak query rate ~100 queries/sec, support 500 concurrent users
other: End-to-end encryption (AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit)
SAML/OIDC SSO integration
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with audit logging
Automated backup and disaster recovery plan
Versioned document ingestion pipeline

inferred_requirements.requirement: Document preprocessing pipeline (OCR, text extraction, semantic chunking, embedding generation) with incremental update support
inferred_requirements.reason: Technical manuals may contain scanned pages, tables, and code snippets; efficient chunking and up-to-date embeddings are essential for retrieval quality and latency.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Vector database with GPU acceleration and horizontal sharding (e.g., Milvus or FAISS on GPU)
inferred_requirements.reason: To achieve sub‑second retrieval at 500 concurrent users over a 50GB corpus, the index must be memory‑resident and searchable in parallel.

inferred_requirements.requirement: LLM serving optimization (TensorRT-LLM or NVIDIA Triton Inference Server) with prompt caching
inferred_requirements.reason: Reduces generation latency to meet the <3s SLA while allowing larger models that lower hallucination rates.

inferred_requirements.requirement: Observability stack (metrics, distributed tracing, alerting) and user feedback loop for continuous hallucination reduction
inferred_requirements.reason: Operational visibility ensures SLA compliance; feedback enables model retraining and prompt refinement to keep hallucination <2%.

gaps.gap: Specific hardware budget or exact GPU model count
gaps.suggestion: Define CAPEX/OPEX limits or target utilization (e.g., 70% GPU avg) to right-size the deployment.

gaps.gap: Update frequency of the PDF technical manuals (e.g., daily, weekly)
gaps.suggestion: Specify ingestion cadence to size the preprocessing pipeline and determine if near‑real‑time updates are needed.

gaps.gap: Composition and size of the hallucination evaluation set
gaps.suggestion: Provide details on domain‑specific Q&A pairs, metrics used (e.g., FEVER, BERTScore), and validation frequency.

gaps.gap: Language requirements (English only, multilingual)
gaps.suggestion: Clarify if translation or multilingual embeddings are required for global users.

gaps.gap: Uptime SLA (e.g., 99.9% monthly)
gaps.suggestion: Add availability target to drive redundancy, load‑balancing, and disaster‑recovery design.

conflicts.conflict: Larger LLMs improve factuality but increase generation latency; achieving both may require aggressive optimization or model distillation.
conflicts.suggestion: Benchmark multiple model sizes (e.g., 7B vs 13B) with TensorRT-LLM; consider a two‑stage approach: fast retrieval + lightweight generator with fallback to larger model for low‑confidence queries.

conflicts.metric: 500 concurrent users vs. On‑premise GPU capacity
conflicts.conflict: A single A100 may not sustain 500 concurrent generation requests at <3s latency without queuing.
conflicts.suggestion: Plan for a GPU cluster (>=4 A100s) or implement request batching and dynamic scaling; alternatively, enforce a soft concurrency limit with queueing and client‑side throttling.

conflicts.conflict: Authentication and authorization checks can add 100‑300ms, eating into the 3s budget.
conflicts.suggestion: Cache validated tokens (e.g., JWT) for short TTL (<5min) and offload auth to a sidecar or API gateway; measure and budget <=150ms for auth in latency SLA.

raw.summary: The project calls for an on‑premise, enterprise‑grade RAG chatbot that can answer questions over a 50GB corpus of technical PDFs with sub‑second retrieval, <3s end‑to‑end response, and hallucination rates under 2% for up to 500 concurrent users. It must enforce SSO and RBAC, provide page‑level citations, meet GDPR/ISO‑27001 compliance, and run without external API calls, requiring a robust preprocessing pipeline, GPU‑accelerated vector store, optimized LLM serving, and comprehensive monitoring to satisfy performance, security, and operational goals.

performance_goals.rationale: Ensures high accuracy for meeting minutes and actionable items
Allows near-real-time transcription for live meeting captioning

performance_goals.target: >=500 meeting hours per day on a single GPU node
performance_goals.rationale: Supports scaling to large organizations with many concurrent meetings

constraints.hardware: NVIDIA A100 40GB GPU (or equivalent) with sufficient CPU RAM for preprocessing
constraints.scale: Support up to 100 concurrent meeting streams, each up to 4 hours length
other: Must operate in a VPC with data-at-rest encryption
Must provide API access for integration with calendar and collaboration tools

inferred_requirements.requirement: Speaker diarization to label who said what
inferred_requirements.reason: Meeting transcripts are useful only when attribution is clear for follow‑up actions

inferred_requirements.requirement: Custom vocabulary / domain‑specific language model (e.g., product names, acronyms)
inferred_requirements.reason: Reduces WER in technical meetings and improves actionable item detection

inferred_requirements.requirement: Secure storage and retention policies for transcripts
inferred_requirements.reason: Compliance with data protection regulations and corporate governance

gaps.gap: Language(s) of the meetings
gaps.suggestion: Specify primary language(s) (e.g., English, multilingual) to select appropriate ASR models

gaps.gap: Audio quality expectations (e.g., microphone type, background noise)
gaps.suggestion: Define required SNR or provide guidelines for recording equipment to ensure target WER

gaps.gap: Real‑time vs batch processing need
gaps.suggestion: Clarify whether live captioning is required or if post‑meeting transcription suffices

conflicts.conflict: High accuracy (low WER) vs low latency
conflicts.suggestion: Use a two‑pass approach: fast first pass for live captioning, second pass with larger model for final transcript

raw.summary: The solution will transcribe enterprise meeting recordings into accurate, speaker‑labeled text with ≤12% WER and near‑real‑time latency, processing hundreds of meeting hours daily on GPU‑accelerated infrastructure while meeting GDPR/CCPA compliance and providing secure, searchable transcripts for downstream workflows.

case.input: Meeting transcription for 10-person video calls, real-time streaming at <500ms latency, speaker diarization, technical vocabulary adaptation (~500 jargon terms), punctuation + capitalization, summarization of key decisions, GDPR-compliant 30-day retention, English + Spanish.
